content.decision_question: Is preserving political stability by avoiding reform-triggered conflict a legitimate goal in itself, even when it means sustained inaction on an officially acknowledged problem, or does that inaction itself constitute an unacceptable erosion of governmental legitimacy?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can document historical patterns of trust erosion following unaddressed ministerial acknowledgments and can trace the coalition dynamics underlying the 1989-90 settlement, but it cannot determine how much political risk a government should be willing to accept to preserve legitimacy, nor can it rank stability against accountability as competing public goods — those remain value judgments for democratic deliberation.
content.tension: Status quo monitoring (T1) requires no new legal authority and avoids triggering the intense political mobilization seen against even modest reform proposals, preserving the fragile cross-ideological coalition that established the 1989-90 settlement; however, this same inaction is documented as eroding public trust when ministerial acknowledgment of problems is paired with no corrective action, creating a legitimacy cost that is qualitatively different from, but potentially as consequential as, the risks of active reform.
content.title: Passive monitoring's legitimacy cost versus the political safety of inaction
value_poles: political stability and avoidance of destabilizing conflict
governmental accountability and responsiveness to acknowledged problems
